title: Energy from fuels
summary: This lesson explores the principles of measuring the energy released from fuels. It focuses on the experimental technique of calorimetry, the calculation of enthalpy of combustion using q=mcΔT, and an analysis of the inherent assumptions and systematic errors in this method.
learningObjectives: Define and calculate the enthalpy of combustion for a fuel from experimental data.
Apply the equation q = mcΔT to calculate the heat energy absorbed by water in a calorimetry experiment.
Analyse the assumptions and major sources of error in simple calorimetry experiments.
Distinguish between the heat energy change (q) and the molar enthalpy change (ΔH).

simpleExplanation.title: Fuels: How Much Bang for Your Burn?
simpleExplanation.summary: We measure the energy released by a fuel by using it to heat up a known amount of water. By measuring the water's temperature change, we can calculate how much energy the fuel provided.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're trying to figure out which snack gives you the most energy. You could eat a snack and then see how long you can run on a treadmill. In chemistry, we 'feed' the energy from a burning fuel to a beaker of water and see how much its temperature 'runs' up. A bigger temperature jump means a more energy-dense fuel.
steps: Set up your calorimeter: Place a known mass of water in a copper can and measure its initial temperature.
Weigh the fuel: Measure the initial mass of the spirit burner containing the fuel (e.g., ethanol).
Heat the water: Light the wick and use the flame to heat the water until its temperature has risen by a significant amount, for example, 20°C.
Measure the final values: Extinguish the flame and immediately record the maximum temperature of the water and the final mass of the spirit burner.

flashcards.front: What is the definition of standard enthalpy of combustion (ΔH_c°)?
flashcards.back: The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is completely combusted in excess oxygen under standard conditions (298 K and 100 kPa).

flashcards.front: What is the formula for calculating heat energy change (q)?
flashcards.back: q = mcΔT, where q is heat energy (J), m is the mass of the substance being heated (g), c is the specific heat capacity (J g⁻¹ K⁻¹), and ΔT is the temperature change (K or °C).

flashcards.front: What are the units for specific heat capacity (c)?
flashcards.back: Joules per gram per Kelvin (J g⁻¹ K⁻¹) or Joules per gram per degree Celsius (J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹).

flashcards.front: Why is the experimental value for enthalpy of combustion usually less exothermic than the theoretical data book value?
flashcards.back: Due to systematic errors, primarily heat loss to the surroundings and incomplete combustion of the fuel.

flashcards.front: What is the specific heat capacity of water that you must know for the IB exam?
flashcards.back: 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹ (or 4.18 J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹). This value is provided in the Data Booklet.

flashcards.front: How do you convert the calculated heat energy (q) in Joules to the molar enthalpy of combustion (ΔH_c) in kJ mol⁻¹?
flashcards.back: 1. Divide q (in J) by 1000 to get kJ. 2. Calculate the moles (n) of fuel burned. 3. Calculate ΔH_c = -q / n. Remember to add the negative sign for an exothermic reaction.

flashcards.front: What is a major assumption made when calculating the heat absorbed by the solution in a simple calorimeter?
flashcards.back: It is assumed that all the heat released by the reaction is absorbed by the water. In reality, heat is also lost to the air, the beaker, the thermometer, etc.

flashcards.front: What is incomplete combustion and how does it affect the experimental result?
flashcards.back: Incomplete combustion occurs when there is insufficient oxygen. It produces carbon monoxide (CO) and/or soot (C) instead of just CO₂. It releases less energy than complete combustion, leading to a less exothermic experimental value for ΔH_c.

flashcards.front: Why is ΔT in Kelvin the same as ΔT in degrees Celsius?
flashcards.back: Because the size of one Kelvin unit is the same as the size of one degree Celsius. A change of 1 K is a change of 1 °C. So, T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15, but ΔT(K) = ΔT(°C).

flashcards.front: What is the sign of ΔH for the combustion of a fuel?
flashcards.back: Negative. Combustion is always an exothermic process, releasing energy into the surroundings.

flashcards.front: In a calorimetry experiment, what mass 'm' should be used in q=mcΔT?
flashcards.back: The mass of the substance being heated, which is typically water. Do not use the mass of the fuel burned in this part of the calculation.

faq.q: Why is the enthalpy of combustion always negative?
faq.a: Enthalpy of combustion refers to a combustion reaction, which is a process that releases heat energy into the surroundings. In chemistry, processes that release energy are defined as exothermic and are assigned a negative sign for their enthalpy change (ΔH).

faq.q: What is the difference between heat (q) and enthalpy (ΔH)?
faq.a: Heat (q) is the total amount of energy transferred in a specific event (e.g., the Joules absorbed by the water in your beaker). Enthalpy change (ΔH) is a molar quantity, representing the heat transferred per mole of reactant under constant pressure. So, q is for your specific experiment, while ΔH is the general value for the reaction on a per-mole basis.

faq.q: How can I improve the accuracy of a simple calorimetry experiment?
faq.a: To improve accuracy, you need to minimise the sources of error. You could use a lid to reduce heat loss from the water surface, add draught shields to protect the flame from breezes, ensure a plentiful supply of oxygen to promote complete combustion, and stir the water to ensure an even temperature distribution.

faq.q: Does it matter if I use °C or K for ΔT?
faq.a: No, it does not matter for the change in temperature (ΔT). Because the size of a Celsius degree is the same as a Kelvin, a change of 10 °C is also a change of 10 K. However, if you are ever using an equation with just T (not ΔT), like the ideal gas law, you must use Kelvin.

sections.content: Fuels are substances that store a significant amount of chemical potential energy, which can be released as heat through combustion. Understanding how to quantify this energy release is crucial for comparing the efficiency of different fuels and for applying the principles of thermochemistry. This lesson will guide you through the experimental and calculation methods used to determine the energy content of fuels.

Calorimetry is the science of measuring heat changes associated with chemical or physical processes. For determining the energy from fuels, we typically use a simple calorimeter, which often consists of a metal can (a good conductor) containing a known mass of water. The fuel is burned below the can, and the heat released by the combustion is absorbed by the water, causing its temperature to rise.

items: The basic principle is that the energy released by the fuel is equal to the energy absorbed by the water.
We measure the mass of water, the initial and final temperatures of the water, and the mass of fuel consumed.
The key calculation relies on the specific heat capacity of water.

sections.content: In this equation, '$q$' represents the heat energy absorbed in Joules (J). '$m$' is the mass of the water in grams (g). '$c$' is the specific heat capacity of water, which is 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹. '$\Delta T$' is the change in temperature of the water (Final Temperature - Initial Temperature) in Kelvin (K) or degrees Celsius (°C).

The value of '$q$' you calculate is the heat energy released by burning a specific mass of fuel. To make a fair comparison between fuels, we standardise this to the energy released per mole, known as the enthalpy of combustion, $\Delta H_c$. This involves a two-step process: first find the heat released, then scale it per mole of fuel.

items: Step 1: Calculate the heat absorbed by the water, $q = mc\Delta T$.
Step 2: Calculate the number of moles (n) of the fuel that were burned (moles = mass / molar mass).
Step 3: Calculate the enthalpy of combustion: $\Delta H_c = -q / n$. The result is usually expressed in kJ mol⁻¹.
The negative sign is crucial: it signifies that combustion is an exothermic process (energy is released).

sections.question: In a laboratory experiment, the combustion of 0.55 g of propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH) caused the temperature of 150 g of water to increase from 22.1 °C to 35.4 °C. Calculate the enthalpy of combustion of propan-1-ol in kJ mol⁻¹.
sections.solution:
Step 1: Calculate the heat energy absorbed by the water (q).

$m_{water}$ = 150 g
$c_{water}$ = 4.18 J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹
$\Delta T$ = 35.4 °C - 22.1 °C = 13.3 °C

$q = mc\Delta T = (150 \text{ g}) \times (4.18 \text{ J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹}) \times (13.3 \text{ °C})$
$q = 8340.3 \text{ J}$ (or 8.34 kJ)

Step 2: Calculate the moles of propan-1-ol burned (n).

Molar mass of propan-1-ol (C₃H₈O) = (3 × 12.01) + (8 × 1.01) + 16.00 = 60.11 g mol⁻¹
Mass burned = 0.55 g

$n = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{Molar Mass}} = \frac{0.55 \text{ g}}{60.11 \text{ g mol⁻¹}} = 0.00915 \text{ mol}$

Step 3: Calculate the molar enthalpy of combustion (ΔH_c).

$\Delta H_c = -\frac{q}{n} = -\frac{8340.3 \text{ J}}{0.00915 \text{ mol}} = -911508 \text{ J mol⁻¹}$

Convert to kJ mol⁻¹ and apply significant figures (3 s.f. from temperature change and mass of fuel):

$\Delta H_c = -912 \text{ kJ mol⁻¹}$

sections.content: Simple calorimetry experiments are prone to significant errors, meaning the experimental value for enthalpy of combustion is often much lower (less exothermic) than the accepted literature value. It is vital to be able to identify and explain these sources of error.

items: Heat loss to the surroundings: This is the largest source of error. Not all the heat from the flame enters the water; much is lost to the air, the stand, and the calorimeter itself.
Incomplete combustion: If oxygen supply is limited, the fuel may not burn completely to CO₂ and H₂O. Formation of soot (C) or carbon monoxide (CO) releases less energy.
Evaporation of fuel: The fuel is volatile and can evaporate from the wick, meaning the measured change in mass is not solely due to combustion.
Assumptions: We assume the specific heat capacity of the solution is the same as pure water and that the heat absorbed by the calorimeter itself is negligible.

sections.content: When asked to compare your calculated experimental value to a theoretical data book value, always state that the experimental value is less exothermic (less negative). Then, you must explain why, referring to specific, well-explained sources of error like heat loss to the surroundings or incomplete combustion. Simply stating 'human error' will not earn any marks.

sections.question: A student burns 1.02 g of ethanol (C₂H₅OH) and heats 200.0 g of water. The temperature rises by 15.5 K. The theoretical standard enthalpy of combustion for ethanol is -1367 kJ mol⁻¹. Calculate the experimental enthalpy of combustion and determine the percentage error.
sections.solution:
Step 1: Calculate heat absorbed by water, q.

$q = mc\Delta T = (200.0 \text{ g}) \times (4.18 \text{ J g⁻¹ K⁻¹}) \times (15.5 \text{ K})$
$q = 12958 \text{ J} = 12.958 \text{ kJ}$

Step 2: Calculate moles of ethanol burned, n.

Molar mass of C₂H₅OH = (2 × 12.01) + (6 × 1.01) + 16.00 = 46.08 g mol⁻¹
$n = \frac{1.02 \text{ g}}{46.08 \text{ g mol⁻¹}} = 0.022135 \text{ mol}$

Step 3: Calculate experimental ΔH_c.

$\Delta H_{c, exp} = -\frac{q}{n} = -\frac{12.958 \text{ kJ}}{0.022135 \text{ mol}} = -585.4 \text{ kJ mol⁻¹}$
To 3 s.f., this is -585 kJ mol⁻¹.

Step 4: Calculate percentage error.

Percentage Error = $\frac{|\text{Theoretical Value} - \text{Experimental Value}|}{|\text{Theoretical Value}|} \times 100\%$

Percentage Error = $\frac{|-1367 - (-585.4)|}{|-1367|} \times 100\%$

Percentage Error = $\frac{781.6}{1367} \times 100\% = 57.2\%$

(The large error is typical for this experiment and highlights the issues of heat loss and incomplete combustion).
